text: In the operating room, the surgeon identifies a pelvic mass located caudal to the uterine body, projecting towards the right pararectal area.

The surgeon identifies and dissects the anterior leaf of the broad ligament down to the bladder, followed by the identification and dissection of the posterior leaf of the broad ligament toward the pelvic sidewall.

The surgeon transects the round ligament, enhancing the view of the pelvic mass. Extensive retroperitoneal dissection is performed at the pelvic brim, extending toward the back of the bladder, with careful identification of the ureter.
The surgeon completely mobilizes the pelvic mass from its stalk, creating a bladder flap that is pushed down off the cervix and upper vagina. The mass is gradually moved to the right pararectal area.

The surgeon identifies the right ureter multiple times during dissection. The pelvic mass is observed to have a cavity forming a fistula connecting to the right lower cervix. The pedicle of this connection is gradually transected, and the specimen is removed.

The surgeon performs copious irrigation of the surgical field, followed by chromoperturbation, which reveals dye flowing through a visible defect. The defect is then repaired using a barbed wire suture.
The surgeon re-approximates the anterior and posterior leafs of the broad ligament to the round ligament using two ovicryl sutures, securing these structures in place.
The specimen is placed into a retrieval bag and extracted through a laparoscopic port. A hysteroscopy is then performed, revealing an intact uterine cavity.
